metadata.description: Learn how to build composable websites with Gazetta. Templates, fragments, pages, targets, and CLI reference.
metadata.title: Documentation — Gazetta

content.heading: Getting Started
content.subheading: Everything you need to build your first Gazetta site.

content.body:
1. Create a site

npx gazetta init my-site && cd my-site

This scaffolds a site with templates, an admin directory, and a home page — and installs dependencies automatically.

2. Start the dev server

npx gazetta dev — site at localhost:3000, CMS editor at localhost:3000/admin

3. Create a template

A template is a pure function: (params) => { html, css, js }. Every template exports a Zod schema for its content shape. Use any framework — React, Svelte, Vue, or plain TypeScript.

4. Create a page

A page is a folder with a page.json manifest listing its template and inline components. The route is derived from the folder path — pages/about/ becomes /about. Or use the CMS editor to create pages visually.

5. Add shared fragments

Fragments live in fragments/ and are referenced with "@" — like "@header". Update once, every page reflects it instantly.

See the full getting started guide for detailed instructions.

content.heading: Templates
content.subheading: Use any framework. The contract is the boundary.

content.heading: Fragments
content.subheading: Shared components that update everywhere at once.

content.body:
Fragments live in fragments/ and are referenced with "@" in page manifests.

Create a fragment:

fragments/header/fragment.json — defines the template, content, and inline components.

Reference in a page:

"@header" in the page's components list. The @ tells Gazetta to look in fragments/.

Update once, reflect everywhere: Change the header fragment — every page that uses "@header" gets the update. No need to re-publish each page.

At the edge: Fragments are independently stored and cached. The worker assembles them into pages at request time using ESI-style composition. Update a fragment in R2 and all pages reflect it on the next request.

Each target has storage (where files go) and an optional worker (what serves them).

Storage types: r2 (Cloudflare R2), s3 (AWS S3, MinIO), filesystem (local), azure-blob

Serving options:

  • gazetta serve — Node production server (VPS, Docker, Fly.io)
  • worker: cloudflare — Cloudflare Workers (edge assembly)

Build (before deploy or serve):

npx gazetta build — builds admin UI + bundles custom editors/fields into dist/admin/

Publish content:

npx gazetta publish — publishes to default target
npx gazetta publish production — specific target

Serve pages:

npx gazetta serve — serves published pages from first target
npx gazetta serve staging -p 8080 — specific target and port

Run admin CMS:

npx gazetta admin — production CMS for content editors (requires gazetta build first)

Deploy Cloudflare Worker (one-time):

npx gazetta build && npx gazetta deploy production

Build and deploy once. Publish as often as you want.

Authentication: Run npx wrangler login for local R2 access. For CI, set CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN. The CLI loads .env from the site directory automatically.

Cache:

browser: 0 — seconds before the browser revalidates (max-age)
edge: 86400 — seconds before the CDN/proxy refetches from storage (s-maxage)

Configure cache.purge to automatically purge the CDN after publish. Pages can override cache in page.json.

ETags are automatic. Browsers get 304 Not Modified for unchanged pages — zero bandwidth even with browser: 0.

npx gazetta dev starts the CMS at /admin alongside your site.

Browse: Pages and fragments in the left sidebar. Click to select.

Edit: Click a component in the tree → editor form appears (auto-generated from the Zod schema). Fragments are editable too — click @header to edit its content, or expand to edit children.

Click-to-edit: Toggle Edit mode in the preview toolbar. Click any component directly in the preview to select and edit it.

Preview: Live preview updates as you type. Device presets (desktop, tablet, mobile) and fullscreen mode available. Template changes hot-reload automatically — edit a .ts or .tsx file and the preview updates via morphdom with no page flash.

Save: Writes changes to disk.

Publish: Select targets and publish — pre-renders and uploads to the target storage.

Fetch: Pull content from a target back into your local site. Useful for recovering from a lost CMS or syncing between environments.

Dark mode: Toggle with the sun/moon icon in the toolbar.

content.heading: Custom Editors
content.subheading: Replace the default form with a custom editing experience.

content.body:
The CMS auto-generates editor forms from your template's Zod schema. For templates that need a richer editing experience, you can create a custom editor that replaces the default form entirely.

Create a custom editor

Add a file at admin/editors/{template-name}.tsx:

// admin/editors/hero.tsx
import React, { useState } from 'react'
import { createRoot } from 'react-dom/client'
import { DefaultEditorForm } from 'gazetta/editor'
import type { EditorMount } from 'gazetta/types'

function HeroEditor({ content, schema, onChange, theme }) {
  const [data, setData] = useState(content)
  const handleChange = (c) => { setData(c); onChange(c) }
  return (
    <div>
      <div style={{ padding: '1.5rem', background: 'linear-gradient(135deg, #667eea, #764ba2)', borderRadius: 8, color: '#fff', textAlign: 'center', marginBottom: '1rem' }}>
        <h2>{data.title || 'Untitled'}</h2>
      </div>
      <DefaultEditorForm schema={schema} content={data} onChange={handleChange} />
    </div>
  )
}

const roots = new WeakMap()
const editor: EditorMount = {
  mount(el, props) {
    const root = createRoot(el); roots.set(el, root)
    root.render(<HeroEditor {...props} />)
  },
  unmount(el) { roots.get(el)?.unmount(); roots.delete(el) },
}
export default editor

How it works

When you select a component in the CMS, Gazetta checks if admin/editors/{template}.tsx exists. If it does, the custom editor is loaded and mounted instead of the default form. If not, the auto-generated @rjsf form is used.

EditorMount contract

Custom editors export an EditorMount object with mount(el, props) and unmount(el) methods — the same micro-frontend pattern used by single-spa. Works with React, Vue, Svelte, or vanilla JS.

The mount function receives:

  • content — current content values
  • schema — JSON Schema for the template
  • theme — 'dark' or 'light'
  • onChange — call when content changes

Embedding the default form

Import DefaultEditorForm from gazetta/editor to embed the auto-generated form inside your custom editor. This gives you custom UI on top (live preview, visual controls) with standard form fields below.

Hot reload

Custom editors are served by Vite in dev mode. Edit your .tsx file and the editor updates via HMR — no page refresh needed.

content.heading: Custom Fields
content.subheading: Create reusable widgets for individual form fields.

content.body:
Custom fields are reusable widgets that replace individual form fields inside the default @rjsf form. Unlike custom editors (which replace the entire form), custom fields target a single property in the schema.

Create a custom field

Add a file at admin/fields/{field-name}.tsx:

// admin/fields/brand-color.tsx
import React, { useState } from 'react'
import { createRoot } from 'react-dom/client'
import type { FieldMount } from 'gazetta/types'

const PRESETS = [
  { label: 'Indigo', value: '#667eea' },
  { label: 'Purple', value: '#764ba2' },
  { label: 'Coral', value: '#f97066' },
]

function BrandColorPicker({ value, theme, onChange }) {
  const [color, setColor] = useState(value || '#667eea')
  const handleChange = (v) => { setColor(v); onChange(v) }
  return (
    <div>
      <div style={{ height: 48, borderRadius: 8, background: color }} />
      <div style={{ display: 'flex', gap: '0.375rem' }}>
        {PRESETS.map(p => (
          <button key={p.value} onClick={() => handleChange(p.value)}
            style={{ width: 28, height: 28, borderRadius: 6, background: p.value, border: 'none', cursor: 'pointer' }} />
        ))}
      </div>
      <input type="color" value={color} onChange={e => handleChange(e.target.value)} />
    </div>
  )
}

const roots = new WeakMap()
const brandColor: FieldMount = {
  mount(el, { value, theme, onChange }) {
    const root = createRoot(el); roots.set(el, root)
    root.render(<BrandColorPicker value={String(value ?? '')} theme={theme} onChange={onChange} />)
  },
  unmount(el) { roots.get(el)?.unmount(); roots.delete(el) },
}
export default brandColor

Reference in a template schema

Use format.field('field-name') in the Zod schema to connect a property to your custom field widget:

// templates/banner/index.ts
import { z } from 'zod'
import { format } from 'gazetta'

export const schema = z.object({
  heading: z.string().describe('Banner heading'),
  background: z.string().meta(format.field('brand-color')).describe('Background color'),
})

The brand-color widget loads automatically when editing a component that uses the banner template. Other fields in the schema use the default form inputs.

FieldMount contract

Custom fields export a FieldMount object with mount(el, props) and unmount(el) methods — the same pattern as custom editors.

The mount function receives:

  • value — current field value
  • schema — JSON Schema for this property
  • theme — 'dark' or 'light'
  • onChange — call when the value changes

Custom fields + custom editors

  • No custom editor — custom fields work inside the default @rjsf form
  • Custom editor with DefaultEditorForm — custom fields work inside the embedded form
  • Custom editor without DefaultEditorForm — custom fields are NOT used (the custom editor controls everything)

Hot reload

Custom fields are served by Vite in dev mode. Edit your .tsx file and the field widget updates via HMR.
